===accordion
===endaccordion
===accordion
===panel: Introduction

## Introduction

![The Emperor](https://cdnb.artstation.com/p/assets/images/images/068/535/583/large/alexander-gering-caesar-legion-s-emperor-full.jpg?1698070110)
*"You don't get it, do you? The weapons I wield are forged from blood, flesh, sinew, bone - mortal stuff. Fragile, even. And yet my Legion obeys me, even unto death. Why? Because they live to serve the greater good, and they know of no alternatives. - Caesar* 

Caesar, Legion's Emperor still feels (as of this writing) an underwritten commander. Despite being a face commander for a precon tied to a major gaming IP, Caesar clocks in as around the 7th most popular Mardu commander on EDHREC. In my opinion, his popularity is only due to rise over the years.

Much like his video game counterpart, Caesar, Legion's Emperor is a brutal assimilator and aggressor. Pax Mardu will only be won with hard fought sacrifice. This commander will sacrifice his own creatures to sprout forth an army of soldiers, and the larger his army gets the more noncombat damage it'll dole out to opponents.

This fast, exciting commander enables Aristocrats strategies, Token strategies, and also bakes in noncombat "burn" damage as a backup win-condition. And on top of that, it also has card draw! What can't our commander do?

***How does Caesar work mechanically?***

Notably, Caesar's abilities trigger on attack - but thankfully does not require Caesar himself be attacking. As long the player is able to proceed to the attack phase and declare attackers generally, one *will* procc Caesar. 

Once attackers are declared and Caesar is procced, you *may * choose to sacrifice one of your own creatures. If you do, you must choose two of three modes to activate:
1. The first allows you to create two 1/1 soldier creature tokens with haste, that enter tapped and attacking; (this is our best mode, and compounds our board state)
2. The next mode allows you to draw a card in exchange for 1 life; (useful most likely in the very early turns when we are searching for card advantage and where mode three is not yet worth proccing)
3. And finally, the third mode allows us to deal direct noncombat damage to an opponent tied to how many creature tokens we control (this is the biggest payoff to going wide with Caesar, and allows direct burn damage to be a backup wincon if a wide board of tokens cannot secure a win fast enough.) 
*  It's important to note that you do not choose modes or a target for Caesar's ability at the time that it triggers. First, you choose to sacrifice a creature - and then a second, 'reflexive' ability triggers. You choose your modes all at once, and if you chose the third mode - you also choose a target for that mode at this point. The triggered ability goes on the stack as normal.
* Caesar's third mode - the direct burn damage directed at a player - counts the number of creature tokens as it resolves. That being said, it counts all your token creatures as they are being sent into combat, but it counts them before blocks are declared. Don't make the mistake of thinking that just because you lost a few soldier tokens to blockers that they aren't part of the total headcount for his third ability! You get to count your tokens and deal damage at instant speed when you create two tapped and attacking soldiers - this all happens before blocks!

**Front Foot Mentality**

This deck wins by 1) going wide and overwhelming with combat damage; 2) pinging individuals and the entire board with burn damage, or 3) draining all our opponents with aristocrats lifedrain. Prioritizing low-CMC token makers, this deck will look scary to the board quick: it will be pumping out small creatures, and assigning burn damage to all players.

This deck wants to be on the front foot - it's aggressive. When playing this deck, do not be surprised if you become an early archenemy. It also means being comfortable attacking players every turn, and assigning burn damage immediately.

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===accordion
===panel: Gameplan

## Gameplan

![The Legion](https://i.redd.it/zi523l9gby4d1.png)
*"Long ago... when taking Denver, I had to face such a challenge. Many died, over many years to claim the city as ours." - Legate Lanius, circa 2281*


***How do I assemble a win?***

Caesar is an aggressive commander - even if your opponents are not familiar with him, once they see a full combat cycle with Caesar online they may make it a priority to remove him from the game. 

Thus, it's important that Caesar not be cast from the command zone without a supporting engine already on board; Caesar himself is an engine for token generation, draw and burn damage. He's also a sac outlet - but he does require having sac fodder in order to work. 

Turns 1 - 4 should be focused on getting the mana base set up to cast Caesar, but it should also prioritize playing a 2-3 CMC token generator. Caesar could easily be cast on turn 4 on curve, and still have a token generated in turns 2 or 3, allowing him to sacrifice one and begin pumping out his troops as early as the turn he comes down. 

Some commanders want to be played on curve - Caesar does not need to be. If you do not have anything worth sacrificing, then Caesar may not want be out of the safety of the command zone. It's more important that Caesar have tokens or creatures that can afford to be sacrificed to kickstart his engine. Without, he's just a 5-CMC 4/4 soldier.

Repetitive token generation is ideal - cards like Bitterblossom, Black Market Connections, Dollmaker's Shop, Mardu Ascendency, Skrelv's Hive, etc. all create or give you the opportunity to create a token each turn or combat. This repetitive generation means Caesar can sack the free token to create two soldiers. If you can attack into a board state that has no blockers, you can safely begin to accrue and compound the number of soldier tokens on the board which directly benefits Caesar's third mode of burn damage that counts tokens.

Impulse token creation is our next best bet, but not as ideal as repetitive generation. Situationally impulse creation can feel amazing, though repetitive sources of bodies are generally preferable for consistency in building our engine. Cards like Martial Coup, Secure the Wastes, Grand Crescendo, will oftentimes show up as spells or creatures that drop a few tokens on cast one-time. These can still provide the value you need, but they are more susceptible to board wipes - making recovering from the wraths more difficult.

***What should I look for when mulliganing?***

Mulligan on less than three mana hands, in our colors we do not have access to ready ramp spells. It's important we have three lands, and rocks are a bonus. Color-fixing should be relatively easy to do, but consider mulliganing if you only have access to one color in your starting hand.

An ideal hand includes at least one creature or token generator - think Bitterblossom, Secure the Wastes, Ophiomancer etc. If you do not have a token generator or creature that can afford to be sacced, you are going to be starting slow if you do not draw into one. The best hand would include one of our most efficient token generators (Bitterblossom, Jadar Ghoulcaller, Opiomancer, Charismatic Conqueror, Skrelv's Hive, Hanweir Garrison, Krenko, etc.) three lands, a mana rock, and a source of card draw (Skullclamp, Pip-Boy, Enduring Innocence, Black Market Connections). 

***What are this deck's strengths and weaknesses?***

Strengths
* This deck can go wide faster than most, and will be swimming in creatures that can quickly overwhelm the board. It provides excellent defense and generates offensive pressure early and often. 
* This commander has an alternate win-condition stapled into his rule box; meaning that even if our go-wide strategy fails, direct noncombat damage (either through direct burn damage in Caesar's text, ETB ping damage, or aristocrats damage) remains a very viable (and sometimes our primary) way to win.
* This deck can be built to specialize on up to three different unique themes: aristocrats, tokens aggro, or burn/group slug damage. It can, arguably, do all three things moderately well or be modified to focus exclusively on one specific mode of victory.

Weaknesses
* This deck is susceptible to board wipes, and is slightly less resilient to recovering from board wipes because Caesar requires creatures to sacrifice before generating more tokens. Because he cannot generate tokens from nothing, this list needs creatures or low-CMC token generation online before it's gameplan begins (or restarts). 
* This deck *can* be very commander-reliant. Caesar provides the value engine and is the primary mechanism that compounds board presence, draws cards, and puts damage on the board. Caesar players will want Caesar to provide value immediately upon entry on the battlefield, and because so many of his abilities are tied to the specific commander he will often attract removal spells which can deflate forward momentum of the deck.

![PeacePipe](https://images-wixmp-ed30a86b8c4ca887773594c2.wixmp.com/f/ef1ae03c-3478-42c6-ac24-32e1ebb5221b/de65njz-d46ca55f-d54c-4ad8-b9e0-f08f2725b81e.jpg?token=eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9..DYZjcpabm3TrpsEGPS6a-cn4keS7ngEYAb2ybYPCZ0M)

**Crossing the ~~Rubicon~~ Colorado (Turn 0)**

In turn zero talk be transparent that this deck leans closer to an 8 than a true "7" in power level (In bracket terms, this deck is a strong bracket 3 deck and maybe borderline bracket 4 ). Overselling is better than underselling, as many players may be unpleasantly surprised if by Turn 6 you have Mondrak and Caesar on the board with the capability to generate three new soldiers tapped and attacking every turn.

This deck can have a strong board state reliably by turns 6 - 9 without interaction. It could probably commit near lethal damage to a single player by turn 9 or 10 without interaction.

The saltiest cards in this list that you may want to disclose include Anointed Procession, Mondrak, Charismatic Conqueror. Be clear that this deck wants to go wide, burn and drain - but does not play stax, control, or go infinite. At the end of the day, it's fair (but brutally efficient Magic).

***Notable Matchups***

**Combo:** Combo decks can be challenging for aggro decks like this one in general because they oftentimes can chain into their win condition faster than we can pile the damage on.

**Stax:** Stax decks are effective quite generally, but the conditions for our deck to function requires a formulaic set-up. A proactive stax effects that keeps creatures off board, or locking down access to mana means that Caesar's engine will never take off. Without it, the deck ends up being a pile of creatures with aristocrat and token synergies - certainly still viable but not enough to keep up.

**Aikido/Pillowfort:** Pillowfort decks alone are not likely going to be a challenge, but a pillowfort deck that is allowed to set up it's layers of tax effects will make attacking into it difficult. Damage redirection, common to aikido, will make the direct damage options risky against these decks. These decks should be prioritized early. 

***Win Conditions***

There are quite a few ways to win with Caesar, both gradually and in splash plays. 

**Aristrocrats Package**: Mirkwood Bats, or really any combo of two companion ETB/death synergies is a strong win-condition. Bats is particularly effective because it pings opponents on both token generation and on the sacrifice in one card, but whenever you can keep Nadier/Zulaport on board alongside Bats/Kambal you will whittle down the board very consistently. Cards like Mondrak and Anointed will accelerate your gameplan, while providing you with a boardstate that can defend you from combat damage as well.

This win condition can be a big play, such as splashing a huge mana dump into Secure the Wastes while Mirkwood is on board, but it typically serves to whittle our opponents down innocuously throughout the game in preparation for our big finishers. 

**Burn Damage**: Caesar's ability to innately throw direct damage at a player compounds in parallel with the go-wide gameplan. Cards such as Warleader's Call, or Goblin Bombardment provide further opportunities to chip or big play splash damage onto opponents directly. Even small techs, such as Commander Mustard or Court of Embereth further the gameplan. Using a mass token generator like Song of Totentanz or Martial Coup while Warleader's Call or Goblin Bombardment are on board can be a strong finisher.

**Combat Damage**: The most obvious way is sometimes also the best way. Obviously Caesar himself is a great enabler of the combat damage path to victory by compounding soldier tokens tapped and attacking, but cards like Adeline, and Anim Pakal take the token generation and offensive pressure to the next level while techs such as MacCready or Company Commander make it difficult to block against our army of small, angry legionnaires. Cards like Sundering Eruption, Akroma's Will, Ruinous Ultimatum, or Song of Totentanz are potent finishers to get our attackers hasted, pumped and or evasive/unblockable. 

===endpanel
===endaccordion

===accordion
===panel: Card Choices and Modifying Direction

## **Deck Techs: Staples, Flexes, & Maybes**

Caesar can be built in a few different ways. It's entirely possible to build him less aggro and more aristocrats (with a small splash of Red). It's possibly to fully embrace go-wide tokens with little to no aristrocrats strategies. 

## *Staples:*

**Adeline, Resplendent Cathar:** A phenomenal 3 CMC token generator, Adeline is a big body that gets bigger alongside your board state. Her ceiling as an attacker is very high, and Vigilance means you get to actually use her 4 toughness against the crackback. In the early game she's generating at least three tokens which are perfect sacrifice bait for Caesar's ability and synergistic with how we want to play the game: on the attack. (Importance: 6/10)

**Anim Pakal, Thousandth Moon**: Another token generator that can be cast before our commander, and one of the best token generators in these colors for aggro decks. Anim Pakal will trigger every turn we go to combat since we have a plethora of non-Gnome attackers. She compounds tokens faster than even Caesar, and in conjunction provides enough sac bait for Caesar with enough leftover to power our burn strategies and combat strategies. (Importance: 7/10)

**Agate Instigator**: Similar to Impact Tremors or Warleader's Call, Instigator burns the whole table per creature entry. With all the token generation in the list, as well as the token generation stapled to Caesar, Instigator can quickly rack up damage against the board. Unlike Warleader's Call, he is suspect to creature removal, but he also has an offspring cost that can be paid to create a copy. For decks that want to rely more heavily on burning the table, Agate Instigator is a worthwhile addition. (Importance: 6/10)

**Commissar Severina Raine:** Severina is a common tech with Caesar because she does everything we want to accomplish. She's a sac outlet that provides us lifegain and card draw in a pinch, letting her run our aristrocrats strategy herself; but more importantly she benefits from an attack trigger that drains the table as well. The larger our token army, the more benefit Severina provides. (Importance: 6/10)

**Enduring Innocence:** An upgrade to Welcoming Vampire, Enduring allows us opportunities to draw cards for executing our normal gameplan. It can survive creature removal and boardwipes, and in a pinch can even be sacrifice fodder for Caesar to start our token engine (all while still providing the same benefits.) (Importance: 6/10)

**Isshin, Two Heavens as One**: One of the best cards in a deck that cares about attack triggers, Isshin effectively doubles Caesar's ability allowing you to create twice as many bodies and burn two opponents. Because the list relies on attack triggers generally, Isshin is one of the best cards you can plan around. (Importance: 9/10)

**Kambal, Profiteering Mayor**: Kambal is similar to Mirkwood Bats, and is part of the aristocrats package we run. His first paragraph is situationally cool, but it's the second paragraph of text that makes him a solid synergy with Caesar's token generation. Because the list bakes in a lot of token generation, Kambal can ping the table all game long and add in some lifegain utility. (Importance: 5/10)

**Loyal Apprentice**: One of the best token generators in red, Loyal Apprentice can attack the turn she's put into play - meaning if she's cast after Caesar is already on the field she can trigger her Lieutenant ability right away and provide sac fodder for Caesar's engine. Because she's only 2 CMC she also is easily cast before Caesar comes down on curve to start the gameplan. (Importance: 7/10)

**Mirkwood Bats**: One of the best aristocrats pieces in our overall package for draining the table. Mirkwood bats pings on creating our tokens (per each token creation), and then pings on the sacrifice. This is incredibly synergistic with Caesar's signature ability, as it proccs three times in one use of his trigger (one for sacrificing, two for each of the soldiers that are created). With Isshin, or with token multipliers like Mondrak this card quickly gets out of control. There's not many like it. (Importance: 9/10)

**Mondrak, Glory Dominus**: An expensive piece that, if affordable or feasible, is a very strong addition to the list. It ups the power level of the deck in games it appears. Token generation is key to pinging the table with burn or combat damage, and Mondrak accelerates both goals immediately. It also is a 4/4 body itself, and is a sac outlet that has built in protection against board wipes or targeted removal. The longer Mondrak stays on the board, the better your chances at security victory. (Importance: 9/10)

**Myrel, Shield of Argive**: Myrel is another expensive card that is worth the investment. They provide token generation that relies on the small soldier subtheme in the list, while also providing a Grand Abolisher type effect that makes your board state very hard to interact with. Myrel helps keep your army strong and protected as it grows. (Importance: 8/10)

**Martial Coup**: A board wipe that targets creatures, Martial Coup also doubles as a vanilla token creation sorcery. It's primary inclusion in the list is as a board wipe that can serve to keep a board state for you; but if you had to use it where X is less than 5, it's acceptable if you needed token soldiers in a pinch in order to kickstart the rest of the deck's synergies. That modality makes it more useful. (Importance: 6/10) 

**Ruinous Ultimatum**: A flavor win, given the printing with Caesar's art, but also a worthy "I-Win-Now" boardwipe. It's not easy to cast, but a late game Ruinous Ultimatum can often times win you the game, given it's a one-sided board wipe that let's you run away with the game; or alternatively one that wipes the slate clean while letting you rebuild for a comeback. (Importance: 6/10)

**Song of Totentanz**: One of the best token generators and haste-makers in red. SoT allows you to dump a large amount of mana into it to create an army of rats that can help overwhelm in combat, or it can be cast for X is 1 or 0 to enable haste. It's a phenomenal card for finishing the game out when you've generated a lot of attackers but need haste to attack into a board state, or for when you need a large army to sacrifice to a Goblin Bombardment, or to trigger Warleader's Call. Flexible, modular, and synergistic. (Importance: 10/10)

**Sundering Eruption**: An underrated card, as of this writing at least, and an MDFC. Play it as a land if you need it as a land, but keep it in hand otherwise as a finishing play. Sundering eruption can blow up your opponent's most problematic land, but then more importantly prevent blocks, helping you secure combat-enabled victories. (Importance: 7/10)

**Akroma's Will**: Not a surprising inclusion - great as a finisher to make your creatures evasive and lethal or great as a method of protecting your board state against a board wipe. Expensive, so if too pricey can be replaced. (Importance: 5/10)

**Call the Coppercoats**: Another form of token generation that does better in Commander given the number of opponents. Can be used to level the board state, and produce defenders in a pinch or be used to create sac fodder or an army to attack with. (Importance: 6/10)

**Teferi's Protection**: An expensive piece that is certainly more a luxury than a necessity - but one that provides invaluable protection against board wipes. Given how important a board state is to this list, it's a worthwhile investment - but can also be replaced by cheaper cards such as Clever Concealment, Flawless Maneuver, etc. (Importance: 6/10)

**Cursed Mirror**: Being in colors that don't ramp often, Cursed Mirror is the only acceptable 3-CMC mana rock that we can tolerate. In the early game if it's needed or affordable, cast it for the ramp only. In the late game if you draw into it, copy the best ETB effect/creature on the board and use it as sac fodder or as a beatstick on the attack. The floor is low but reliable, the ceiling for this card is quite high however - especially in matchups against creature heavy opponents. (Importance: 5/10)

**Anointed Procession**: One of the best cards in the list, and one of the most expensive, this is a justifiable investment in a list that relies so heavily on maximizing token generation. Anointed will double the amount of soldiers that enter tapped and attacking which enables the combat gameplan, the aristocrats token-gen effort, and the burn damage effect. It will double treasures we create, a common red effect and subtheme in this list, and it will be hard to remove as an enchantment. Definitely not in every budget, but if you already own a copy or can proxy it is an inclusion that significantly ups the power level of the list. (Importance: 10/10)

**Bitterblossom**: Still one of the premier token generators in black, Bitterblossom is reliable and consistent. It'll create evasive attackers, but more importantly will also create sac fodder as early as turn 2; enabling our strategy and for our commander to be cast on curve. The faeries that are generated can be sacked each turn to create our soldier army, be used to power our burn effects, or make for evasive attackers in their own right. (Importance: 9/10)

**Black Market Connections**: A wonderfully modular and flexible card, BMC lets use fix our mana with treasures, gives us much needed card draw, or can be used in a pinch to generate tokens for fuel Caesar's abilities. It does everything we need a card to do and does it in one Enchantment that will be difficult for most opponents to remove. An incredibly solid addition, not easily replaced. (Importance: 8/10)

**Court of Embereth**: Court of Embereth does everything Caesar does; creates tokens, doles out burn damage, draws us cards (through the monarch). It's a strong card, even as 4 CMC, and introducing the monarch mechanic is a very worthwhile effect even if we do not keep the monarch for long - as it serves to shift attention away from our board state once we lose the monarch. More importantly, it keeps generating tokens that can be sacked or used on the attack - and in games where none of our opponents can attack into our board we accelerate the burn damage done against them. (Importance: 7/10)

**Dollmaker's Shop // Porcelain Gallery**: Dollmaker's Shop is the better early unlock Room - offering a 2-CMC token generator on attack (similar to Loyal Apprentice or Mardu Ascendancy) that can be sacrificed to Caesar's ability. In the late game, Porcelain Gallery is a very powerful finishing effect - allowing us to pump our army's power to big levels.  (Importance: 7/10) 

**Goblin Bombardment**: Not much needs to be said, a long running staple in token decks, Goblin Bombardment makes our army board-wipe proof in that it allows you to threaten significant direct damage against any player at instant speed. It can be used to threaten players who would cast board wipes, finish games outright, serve as another form of creature removal or play into our constant pings across the board. No other card really does what it does, and it's an incredibly flexible and powerful tool in a go-wide deck. (Importance: 10/10)

**Reconnaissance**: A pet card of mine; after a much needed reprint in Assassin's Creed, the card has become more readily available. At it's lowest floor, it's a 1-CMC enchantment that serves to give all your attacking creatures pseudo-vigilance, but letting you withdraw them from combat before the tap-stage. At it's ceiling, it's an incredibly flexible tool that let's you attack with a creature you are not willing to lose (like Caesar himself) in order to trigger attack triggers, before withdrawing those creatures to safety. In an attack trigger, combat-heavy deck this card deserves a slot as a staple. (Importance: 10/10)

**Warleader's Call**: An ETB burn effect, like Impact Tremors, the card offers a bit of a slight upgrade by also serving as a blanket anthem effect that makes your small power attackers slightly larger. The longer this card stays on the field, the more value it accrues you by increasing damage in combat and in opportunities to burn the table. (Importance: 8/10)

 
## *Flex Slots, Upgrades and Maybeboard*

**Rabble Rousing**: A token multiplier, Rabble Rousing will create tons of small guys that enable all the token synergies in the deck while also letting us cheat out a card through the hideaway mechanic. At 5-CMC it's a bit overcosted, but it can be a valuable contribution to a list that maximizes token generation and an attack-centric style.

**Skrelv's Hive**: Bitterblossom but in white, Skrelv's Hive is another option for an alterative to Bitterblossom. While the tokens they generate do not have flying, they do have infect and the possibility to grab lifelink later too. For sacrifice fodder, Hive is just as good as BB. For attackers, it's a trade-off. The infect and lifelink will rarely be relevant, but where tokens can be multiplied, then Hive may have the possibility of overwhelming combat-weak decks with poison counters. 

**Arabella, Abandoned Doll**: Operating similalry to Severina, Arabella will enable both burn damage and combat damage by relying on the small tokens created by Caesar's ability. Arabella also offers lifegain, a rare advantage over many of the alternative attackers. If you can find space for her, she's a worthwhile addition to the suite of token/attack triggers synergies. 

**Esper Sentinel**: An upgrade piece that costs a lot, Esper Sentinel offers us more opportunities to draw cards - something this list could always use help with. It also fits into the soldier subtheme.

**Krenko, Tin Street Kingpin**: Similarly to Anim Pakal, Krenko [Tin Street Kingpin] helps compound the board state by pumping out goblins. He'll become a very strong attacker in his own right, similar to Anim Pakal, while providing great token generation and offensive pressure. 

**Mahadi, Emporium Master**: This list could be modified to lean even more heavily into treasure generation, and Mahadi is a great bridge piece. Still fitting neatly into an aristrocrats package, Mahadi has the upside of creating tons of treasures for not only the creatures that are sacrificed but also killed in combat. The value these treasures provide over time could help pay off bigger finishing plays in the late game, with additional tech cards such as Insurrection or City on Fire, for example. 

**Ojer Taq, Deepest Foundation**: An expensive upgrade piece in a vein similar to Mondrak, Ojer Taq is the only card that triples token generation, taking this deck's power level another notch. It will draw a lot of removal towards it, but at it's floor serves as a land that can be flipped back to Ojer Taq. A win the game more type of card. 

**Roaming Throne**: Similar to Isshin, this card doubles triggers - but all of them. A strict upgrade from Isshin, RT would allow us to benefit from death triggers, attack triggers, ETB triggers, all in one card that can also fit into the soldier subtheme. A very strong addition and upgrade that will be certain to draw plenty of targeted removal.

**Teysa Karlov**: For a deck direction that wanted to pivot even more towards a traditional aristocrats list, Teysa's addition is mandatory as it multiplies and accelerates the death triggers suite of cards in this list. 

**Boros Charm**: An affordable, very splashable, modular spell that offers protection for our board state or pump/burn effects, both useful to our overall gameplan. 

**Grand Crescendo**: Another great upgrade piece that protects the board state from board wipes, with the option to create even more tokens with an X-cost. Flexible, can be used like a Dawn's Truce/Flawless Maneuver, or dumped with mana to create a huge amount of tokens to close out a game. 

**Inkshield**: An upgrade piece that offers a protection and redirection against the crackback against opponents swinging into us; preventing damage and generating an evasive army instead. This kind of Aikido-esque card swings games from losses to wins when timed right. 

**Elas il-Kor, Sadistic Pilgrim**: A good tech for taking the list further into the aristocrats archetype. Elas il-Kor pings on creature ETB and on creature death triggers, much like a less flexible Mirkwood bats. It's a phenomenal piece in a deck that pumps out creature tokens and also causes a lot of creature death triggers - add it along with Tesya Karlov to a list that wants to go even more heavily into the aristocrat direction.

**Dolmen Gate**: A great piece that, similar to Reconnaissance, prevents losing attackers to combat damage. It allows us to use and enjoy our attack triggers without fear of dropping thetoken creature count we need to fuel Caesar's burn ability, or to defend ourselves with blockers from crackback attackers.

**Caretaker's Talent**: An upgrade, in many ways, from Enduring Innocence/Welcoming Vampire in that this enchantment also comes with a blanket anthem effect and a copy ability - both very affordable and effective in this list. Providing card draw and anthem effects for executing only our game plan, a worthy addition in any token deck. 

**City on Fire**:  A high-mana finishing piece that luckily has Convoke, meaning our many tokens can help pay down the cost to cast. City on Fire triples damage, meaning when it comes down the game must be won immediately, or an enormous target will be painted on the player's back. Incredibly potent as a finisher. 

**Three Tree City**: A strong, pricey upgrade land that plays into the Human/Soldier subtheme, letting us ramp and multiply the mana we have available to us in late game. 

* **Fervent Charge**: Fervent Charge is an okay enchantment that comes out of the box for the base precon. It's a 4 CMC enchantment that gives attacking creatures a blanket +2/+2 boost, which is not insignificant in a go-wide deck. It's main issues are that it's cost is a moderately high for a benefit that does not help your soldiers when blocking. It also does not assist Caesar's triggered tokens, which come in tapped and attacking. Overall it can really help pile on damage in a combat-heavy game, but it's utility is narrow and may be better replaced by a damage multiplication effect like City on Fire - which would also benefit our aristocrat and burn damage effects rather than just our combat damage. 

![Propoganda](https://i.etsystatic.com/53882963/r/il/9ad496/6448659647/il_570xN.6448659647_juo0.jpg)
 
## *The Hotlist*

*Cards that are being considered for replacement, pending playtesting or analysis:*

* **Hanweir Garrison:** A card that was added to fit the soldier subtheme, while also generating tokens for a low mana cost. Hanweir also works with it's companion land card, Hanweir Battlements, which creates a creature with even bigger tokens. As this list moves further and further from the soldier subtheme, Hanweir looks more replaceable with a better token-generator on attack trigger, like Krenko Tin Street Kingpin, who could easily eclipse Haneweir in how many troops it can generate.

* **Hour of Reckoning:** A decent boardwipe in a token heavy deck, that serves as a one-sided boardwipe with convoke. That being said, it only hits creatures, meaning this list only has one boardwipe that hits other permanents and it's quite expensive depsite the convoke utility. May be replaced later with a more modular board-wipe like Farewell, or Austere Command. Farewell hits whatever we want it to hit, and is one of the best wipes in white. Austere Command can hit big creatures, keeping our little guys alive and hits other types of permanents.
* **Crackling Doom**: A nifty removal spell that hits each opponents most powerful creature and also zings them for 2 damage. At 3 mana (R/W/B) it's quite mana pricey and can't target the creature we may want to remove most. Given how few options we have for targeted non-creature removal, this card may soon be replaced by a more generic removal spell like Despark or Generous Gift.
* **Mardu Ascendancy**: Like Fervent Charge, Mardu Ascendency is a decent enchantment that wants 3 different color pips in Mardu colors. It's primary purpose is repetitive token generation, which is accomplishes well. It has additional utility in that it can be sacked to give a one time boost to toughness which can help your board survive certain removal spells or board-wipes like Toxic Deluge, etc. It's only downside is the inefficiency of it's mana requirements and how it may be tricker to cast as your early-game token generator - in this regard, it may be easier to replace with a card like Skrelv's Hive or Chivalric Alliance. Skrelv makes tokens more efficiently for sack fodder, but provides little else. Chivalric Alliance is more efficient, and provides card draw on the attack - and it can generate tokens for an additional mana cost.

## *The Watchlist*

*Cards that are being considered as additions or upgrades, pending open space or budget:*

* **General Kreat, the Boltbringer:** A fantastic addition and upgrade to this deck. Kreat is both an Impact Tremors effect and a token maker, with both soldier and goblin synergies. He would be great if bundled with Krenko (see below) since he would create more tokens than just one from his own attack - but he's valuable enough as it is just for his Impact effect.
* **Krenko, Tin Street Kingpin:** A card that I have been circling for this list for some time, (weaker) Krenko is basically a mono-red Anim Pakal. He buffs himself and is guaranteed to make two tokens at least - which make for great sac fodder but could also make for a great combat engine. When paired with the ETB burn effects, he could quickly whittle the whole board while threatening offensive pressure. If Krenko comes in, definitely add Kreat at the same time to keep the goblin subtheme and synergy (which is also an argument for maybe keeping Mardu Ascendancy).


===endpanel

===panel: Updates (Tap the carat to collapse and skip if you're new here!)
![Updates](https://64.media.tumblr.com/b36e6237be77621a512b1078653b3068/956fe4af8443be0f-ea/s1280x1920/3a5e044ece2e0ff6533fed2e259eeb0fdabf218d.jpg)
## Updates & Changelog

End of Year Changelog (2025):

Final changes:

Final swaps aren't coming from most recent sets; but just dropping cards that more often than not feel "dead" in hand. 

- Swapping Solemn Simulacrum for Trouble in Pairs: Solemn was a decent mainstay that survived many rounds of cuts before, because it is a rare piece of land ramp in our colors that also wants to be sacrificed for value. But at four-mana, we want more "oomph" in a list that promotes efficiency and in a mana-slot that competes with our Commander. The list still struggles with pure card draw/card advantage, so we take one of the best in class card draw effects in white instead.
- Swapping Pip-Boy 3000 for Plunge into Darkness: Pip-Boy 3000 is very cool, a multi-tool that leaned into our attack triggers to get some relative value in card selection/filtering, or untapping lands. Unfortunately, I realized it was a bit too slow and didn't do much for me most games. Plunge is a very cool card that is modal - it is a powerful sac-outlet that can end the game if we have an aristocrat engine up, or just net us some lifegain to make the list more resilient. But it has even better value in that it allows us impulse card selection - possibly letting us dig into our library to search out a win-condition.

-----

1. Added Weftstalker Ardent to the Watchlist
- Another one of the creature-ETB effects, but in this case may be an upgrade to some of the others we run. Warp means we can flash the card in for cheap, using the rebate on the discounted cost to pump more into an X spell for creating a massive board ETB burn, before safely tucking the card away to be brought back later from exile.

2. Added Haliya, Guided by Light to the Watchlist
- In the same light of creature-ETB effects that wants to benefit from many tokens, Haliya offers the chance to make the deck more resilient by adding in some lifegain in a Soul Warden effect, with card advantage stapled into the box as a payoff. Not sure if there is space for it, but if there is this could be an interesting evolution for the deck for survivability. 

3. Added Exalted Sunborn to the Watchlist
- Another token doubler - because we are already running Mondrak/Anointed, I don't think Exalted makes the list since I am doubtful that it's better than Mondrak. Analysis on this one is ongoing. 

4. Added Cosmogrand Zenith to the Watchlist
- A flexible value creature that can do a couple of important things for the deck. She's a token generator, a soldier that generates more soldiers (which is always nice if we want to bring back the soldier subtheme), and more importantly gives us a way to buff the board once it is wide enough. We don't have a ton of effects that do that, so it's a tempting addition - though the way this deck has been operating as of late, it functionally feels more burn/aristocrat than it does a go-wide combat deck. 

5. Added Restoration Magic to the Watchlist
- A worthy consideration for our protection suite. For one pip it protects our commander (a card that is a popular removal target), for more it protects our whole board in the late game. Strongly considering. 

6. Added Summon: Knights of the Round to the Watchlist
- Splashy, big spells isn't the direction I have taken this list but if I had to add one I suppose this would be a decent one. It dumps huge amounts of token bodies onto the field which we can use for Caesar burn numbers, sacrifice fodder, defense and/or offense before the card's largest payoff - setting up a huge turn for a big swing and wrath-proofing the board. Downsides here: it's (mana & dollar-value) expensive, it's extremely telegraphed, and as a creature not too hard to remove. 

7. Added SOLDIER Military Program to the Watchlist
- A value enchantment that wants Caesar out - it generates tokens, adds to the soldier subtheme, and pumps our board similarly to Cosmogrand at a much slower clip. Could be a decent addition, analysis ongoing/

8. Added Elas-il Kor, replacing Corpse Knight
- Easy upgrade, Elas-il Kor doing more for the same amount of pips.

Tarkir Dragonstorm Changelog:

1. Added Within Range to the Watchlist (3/26/2025)
- A new card released in the Mardu precon from Tarkir, Within Range is a 4-CMC enchantment that does two things we want: (A) it makes tokens (two warriors), and (2) it's a pay-off for our aggro playstyle, dealing direct life loss to opponents equal to the number of creatures attacking them. It's certainly a great finisher in the late game, but it even has viability dropping in the early-game to kickstart our token-sacrifice engine. The only issue is that at 4-CMC it enters a very crowded part of the mana curve. 

2. Added Ainok Strike Leader to the deck
- Another card releasing in the Mardu precon Mardu surge, Ainok offers fantastic value as a 2-CMC creature that creates 1/1 goblin's tapped and attacking per opponent. Operating like a mini-Adeline, it also offers a defensive upside with it's self-sacrifice ability protecting your creature tokens by making them indestructible. A very strong choice that I am leaning on picking up and finding a space for.

3. Added Zurgo Stormrender to the deck
- The alternate commander from the Mardu Surge precon, Zurgo is a 3-CMC body with Mobilize 1 - creating a tapped and attacking warrior token on attack that sacrifices at the end step. Zurgo also draws you cards (if the token leaves the battlefield while attacking - which synergizes perfectly with Caesar's attack ability). If the token isn't lost while attacking but lost at any other time, Zurgo pings each opponent for 1. He effectively supports the aggro token synergy and the aristocrats package at the same time. While he does cost one red, black and white to cast - he may be a very worthwhile token-generator that comes down before Caesar does.

4. Added Windcrag Siege to the deck
- This is a phenomenal new Boros staple enchantment that allows you to either 1) double attack triggers, or 2) create recurring creature tokens with lifelink and haste. An absolute win-win in this deck. Windcrag synergizes incredibly well with all the "attack" trigger creatures in our deck, but even if at it floor it triggers Caesar twice that is an incredibly powerful effect that lets us go wide faster, burn faster, and gain card advantage faster. At it's floor, if Caesar has been hated out of the game, it can generate attackers/tokens that other cards may be able to use in our token synergy.

Aetherdrift Changelog

1. Added Priest of the Crossing to the Watchlist (2/9/2025)
- A new card that released in the Eternal Might precon, Priest of the Crossing is an interesting aristocrats piece that absolutely could earn a spot in a Caesar list. It's static effects hands out +1/+1 counters to all our creatures for every creature under our control that dies *per turn, including our opponents' turns.* This means that if we sacrifice at least one soldier on our turn, we're putting a +1/+1 counter on everything we control - but more realistically we are losing more soldier tokens in combat and then adding even more counters to our army. After one whole turn cycle, especially if we lose tokens to blocks, we can effectively turn our wide army quite tall as well.

2. Added Bleachbone Verge to the Watchlist (A better replacement for Isolated Chapel)

1/12/25 Changelog:

1. Replaced Boros Charm with Grand Crescendo (1/12/25)

- Grand Crescendo is a better use of our mana than Boros Charm, even if it includes more mana to pay into it. Generally, we aren't using Boros Charm for the double-strike ability since we are carrying many smaller creatures in the deck that have less utility for it. At it's floor, it also let's up mana dump Citizens out which can serve as blockers or fodder, or as a massive triggering of our ETB pinger effects. 

2. Replaced Shadowblood Ridge with Sunscorched Divide
- Adding even more white to our pip count, we swap a land to ensure there's enough access to white mana.

3. Replaced Fervent Charge with Solphim, Mayhem Dominus
- The enchantment Fervent Charge never did enough for us. Solphim let's us pump huge numbers with our noncombat damage, seeing as how difficult it can be to get past blockers. While Fervent is a big boost to combat, Solphim is a big boost to burn damage package and the aristrocrats drain package so we'll keep Fervent in Sideboard for now.​

1/5/25: Created the updates column within the primer to better track gradual upgrades and tweaks to reflect the changing direction of the deck.

1/5/25 Changelog:

1. Replaced Captain of the Watch for Battle Angels of Tyr 

- In many cases a straight upgrade. Captain of the Watch provided synergies as a soldier-type lord and impulse token generator, but for 6 CMC she was far too expensive and comes far too late to be useful in games. Battle Angels allows us to get a token generator in one turn earlier, and they additionally provide card draw, ramp, and lifegain. While Myriad tokens do not stay on the battlefield, they can still be sacrificed as they enter tapped and attacking for Caesar's ability and the cards upside is high enough that it doesn't matter we don't get to keep them.

2. Replaced Wear // Tear with Untimely Malfunction

* Trading away the ability to remove enchantments from the deck for an even more modular card. Wear & Tear isn't a bad card, but Untimely Malfunction let's us keep artifact removal as an option while also letting us have the option to  redirect a single spell or stop blocks. In a combat heavy deck that doesn't have any other counterspells, this is a worthy addition that we will have to accept we do not have dedicated enchantment removal. (Though it does beg the question if we should consider adding Generous Gift or Despark down the line). 

3. Replaced Commander Mustard with Agate Instigator

* Removing Captain of the Watch opened the floodgate to considering moving this deck away from the soldier subtheme a bit more, and so I decided to remove the other lord in the deck for soldiers. While Mustard could make our soldiers infinitely better, he's a 5-drop that has an additional activated cost to get the most out of his ability. He's too expensive and too slow for what we want to do, even if trample/vigilance are hard to give up - they were hardly ever relevant. 
* Agate Instigator leans the deck closer to the burn damage - and is basically Purphorous or Warleader's Call on a body. At it's floor, it's a cheap creature that will ping the table like an Impact Tremors but at it's ceiling we can spend as much mana as we would on Mustard to double the effect with Offspring. I wanted to find a spot for this guy, and moving away from the kindred synergy let us find a spot for him.

4. Replaced Entrapment Maneuver with Boros Charm

* Not really an upgrade since the cards serve different purposes but a side-grade. Boros Charm is one of the best, most splashable modular spells in these colors. It gives us board state protection, pump, or can help us close out a game with a little extra burn damage. Entrapment Maneuver is too overcosted to be useful, when am I ever passing a turn with that much mana open in this list? 

5. Replaced General's Enforcer with Mother of Runes

* A straight upgrade to our protection suite. General's Enforcer was there to keep Caesar safe, but for the most part indestructible isn't as useful as it seems. Mom let's us protect Caesar, but really any one else, and protects against more types of removal while also lowering our curve. She doesn't have incidental token generation, but we hardly ever used Enforcer for his tokens; meanwhile Mom can also help us get past blockers, working like Reconnaissance when we need to attack with Caesar himself. 

6. Replaced Assemble the Legion with Dollmaker's Shop // Porcelain Gallery

* This was the hardest cut to make flavor/theme wise; the Fallout printing of Assemble the Legion is perfect in this deck. Unfortunately, it's just a card that has been powercrept out of high-powered casual EDH, in my opinion. 

* At 5-CMC it's too slow to compete with our other options; and while it does compound tokens at a better rate, it comes online so late in the game that we are likely losing if we had to rely on Assemble the Legion. In longer games that stagnate it can shine, certainly, but most games do not seem to go that long in playtesting. If there's space for this card, I'll find another spot for it in the future possibly because it hurts to cut such an on-theme card.
* Dollmaker's Shop // Porcelain Gallery is a Room type Enchantment from Duskmourne that offers us modularity (my favorite) and it's first Room (Dollmaker's Shop) comes in as early as turn two. It operates like Mardu Ascendancy and creates a token on attack, so as long as we can get an attacker online before Caesar hits the field on turn four we can get the engine going. It's secondary room is all upside for us; if we get it unlocked in late game it can boost our attackers to absurdly high power levels for our go-wide combat strategy.

7. Replaced Memorial to Glory with Elegant Parlor

* Straight upgrade of a bad land to a good surveil land that helps us draw through the deck faster and is searchable with our fetches. We lose some token generation, again, but on a card that we never want to use for tokens.

8. Replaced Nomad Outpost with Arid Mesa

* Nomad Outpost is the worst triland we can remove, since it comes in tapped and has no other bonuses that come with it (not being searchable or fetchable either.) Path of Ancestry was considered, given the loss of some of the soldier synergy, but PoA still let's us scry when we cast Caesar and has access to all our colors too - so for now it should stay.

9. Replaced Myriad Landscape with Solemn Simulacrum

* Having worked hard to lower the mana curve of the deck, I feel comfortable dropping from 38 lands to 37. Myriad is the worst land we have to cut, and we won't miss it's modest ramp too much if we replace it with another ramp piece like Solemn. Better than Myriad, Solemn is a ramp piece that wants to be sacrificed (serving our aristocrats/Caesar package) while also giving us free card draw. Neat.

10. Replaced Windbrisk Heights with Eiganjo, Seat of the Empire

* A straight upgrade, Windbrisk isn't worth the slot it eats. It's a tapland that let's us cheat out a card, but our mana curve is low enough we don't need to cheat anything out. Eiganjo is a better white mana pip that doesn't come in tapped, and is a removal spell if we need it.
